speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three with Elisabeth Mann. We were talking about any valuables that you tried to keep that you had taken to the ghetto, or to the camp. Was there anything of significant value?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or I found it [INAUDIBLE] in that place. When we arrived to the ghetto and brought in many things-- what we thought we will be able to use wherever we go, like toothpaste, where we put in money. Many people brought in books. The German SS ordered us young people to go there and collect everything and put it in a certain room, carrying every so-called merchandise, whatever the Jews brought in for their own use. And between all of the wonderful things what I saw there, I just rescued-- stole a book, because, for me, I loved to read. And that's-- I wanted to read that book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What book was it? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was Sinclair's or something like that. Yeah, and that was another book what really shook me up, also an American book. That was the Hungarian title what I remember the [INAUDIBLE]. You know, like a wild animal who was chased, and the Hungarian translated-- translation that was the title of the book, And I wanted to read that, and I started to read that in the ghetto and in the-- the bricks factory. That was, for me, the most important value then. I was always very idealistic. I always believed in goodness and beauty. I don't know how I could look for beauty in books in that certain situation where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to jump ahead now and talk about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the conditions of the concentration camp. First, I would like for you to describe for me the barracks and, if you recall, the number of the barracks you were in in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were-- when we arrived to Auschwitz and, finally, we came to our barracks-- they were wooden barracks. They had windows but no glass panel in it. And the whole place was just an empty-- excuse me-- empty open territory with a lot of wooden barracks and three sides that was fenced around with electrified wire, one side that wasn't. And there there's the toilet, what was a big hole in the ground and wood panel-- one wood panel thrown across of that tremendous big hole. And that was our toilet, and where we had to go. Not far from that, an SS man and a German shepherd was always standing. Many people from that wood panel fell into that toilet, but nobody ever even tried to help them. For the inmates, there was no way to help them. And other-- the German, naturally, wouldn't help. The goal for them was to be-- to kill all the Jews, so not to save them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people that fell in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died there. Drowned in it. The situation in this camp was-- that was in Auschwitz. It was horrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- How was the barracks set up, as far as how many people were in it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who you shared your space with? The The-- the barracks set-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the barracks were set up for hundreds. And I don't know how many people fit into the barrack, but that was a few hundred people. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had-- every morning, we had to go to roll call, standing [INAUDIBLE] till the SS men came. And every evening, the very same thing was repeated. How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roll call? Three, four hours till they came, and evening the same thing. Many people fainted. I did-- fainted at a couple of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you when you fainted?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were washing me up. I mean, not washing, but pulling me up. Because if you were still on the ground when the SS men came, then you were sent to the crematorium right away. There was no question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time would roll call start in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was still dark. We had no watch or anything, but that was still dark. And after every roll call in the morning, we got the so-called coffee, what was just black water. Five people were in a row, like soldiers. And the first one had an empty can. Previously, there was the gas in it, what they used in the crematorium. And then they ladled in to that can a couple of ladle-- water-- black water. And we got our coffee, every one of us. Drunk five sips, and then the next one and next one, till that was empty. Sometimes we never got more than five sips. That was the whole day, water supply and food supply. And evening, we had, again, the roll call and, that time, food-- so-called soup, what was similar to the black coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tree branches, tree leaves. Sometimes some lucky people got cabbages in it-- I never did-- and a piece of bread like my palm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God. I never knew, and I still don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dark gray, and like clay. First week, I made statues out of it, because I couldn't swallow. But next, after that, I learned to eat. And that was our food for the whole year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get any other kind of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the barracks, what did you sleep on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't sleep on anything. We were so many that we couldn't even lay down. So we sat. And with each other's backs, we sat and supported each other. You know, somebody was behind my back, and so on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the way we managed to sleep, because you did learn to sleep also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it men and women? Or was it just women in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just woman. Just woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of contact did the women have with the men in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And just Hungarian woman, because that was the Hungarian transport, which I was arriving. And in the very beginning few weeks I did go to-- from barracks to barracks always telling that we will be free. Don't give up, because the situation was devastating. What we have seen night and day-- the smoke-covered sky, and the flames from the crematorium, and nothing else. There was not even a grass on the ground, just the dried mud. And that was horrible-- emotionally, physically, and mentally-- for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of communication did you have with other groups of inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Poles or with any other-- did you have any kind of contact with anybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: --other than Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much communication, because we didn't speak Yiddish. And they didn't speak Hungarian. So that was very, very limited, to talk to each other. And in the very beginning, for months and months, we were not together with Polish people, because they were separate groups. Later, when people were selected out and was brought to different part of the camps or different part of I don't know where to work, there they met Polish, or French, or Greek, or whatever nationalities inmates. And we tried. Mostly we spoke German to each other, because that was their only language. But maybe both groups could somehow master it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the other inmates having come in in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very angry. The Polish inmates were very angry at us, first because we didn't speak Yiddish, second, because they said they were three, four years out in the camp, and we had such wonderful life in Hungary. But slowly these feelings disappeared between us. And after the war, we all was friends, even, you know, we worked together with Polish Jews and so on. Jews are Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you initially react to that treatment from the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't react any way, because we couldn't. What can we-- we couldn't even say anything, because we couldn't speak to them. You know, we had no language.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did you communicate to each other amongst your own group about the treatment of the other inmates toward the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: You told whatever you could in a few words that you are not nice, or whatever. I don't know. We didn't beat each other, if that what you think.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean how were you coping with that emotionally?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not so horrible at all. We had so many other things to cope with that we didn't make a big issue of it. And that was-- I am talking about personally, you know. I was not exposed to any other group for a long, long, long time when I was in a concentration camp, just with Hungarian people or people with whom I could talk. And then we had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of hygiene did you practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my. Nothing-- we had no hygiene problem, because we had no water. We had no clothes to change. We had no toothbrush or things like that, so that was no question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that you did to try to clean yourself, any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, when we had the smallest chance to get a drop of water, we washed our hands, or our face, or taking a bath when we could. But as a daily basis, you couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the water when you could?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they took us back to Auschwitz where we arrived to the crematorium, for some reason, they took us a few times back for showers. And then we washed ourselves. And then sprayed us with some yellow stuff-- so-called disinfection lotion, what was very horrible, because they were pinching our skin. But sometime-- I don't know how, maybe it rained-- one time, I collected water in a dish, and I started to wash myself. I don't know how, an SS man-- and that was inside the barracks, but no glass panel-- an SS man saw me. I didn't saw him, and came in, took the water, poured it out, and beat me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't ever take it tragically. Somehow you get used to the idea that you have no rights, or you cannot do anything about anything. And you was beaten, that was the normal things. Not the normal feeling, because you felt the pain, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any disease or epidemic rampant in the camp or in your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: They inflicted us with typhus. I had typhus too, and malaria, and diphtheria, different kind of sicknesses. And naturally, later-- many months later-- I heard there were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] other people infected by them, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They wanted us to die. So there was no problem if you had diphtheria or malaria. I never got in hospital in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you observe or attempt to observe any kind of religious activities or any kind of religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were not any activity. You prayed, yourself, quietly, but not openly. There was no need to it. Every single person could pray. So nobody hurt the person. And that was it. Naturally, I never gave up hope, and never gave up God. And I was praying every morning and evening my little player, what my mother taught me from-- I don't know-- three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What prayer was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was no question about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yom Kippur, or Rosh Hashanah, or things like that, if that's what you are asking. Did anybody observe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did in the summer, 1944. That was Tisha B'Av, you know, when the temple was ruined in Jerusalem. And I wouldn't eat-- wouldn't eat. We had just coffee in the morning-- that black water-- and evening, that slice of bread and so-called soup. And I didn't eat-- smart me-- in concentration camp, when your life depended in a bite of bread. But I didn't eat, because that was Tisha B'Av. If that's what you are referring to, yeah. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was it. I mean, the girls didn't come together to make a minyan, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any point that you ever questioned God?

speaker: interviewee
text: To ask if any god existing? No. I always knew there is a God. But I questioned very, very much why he doesn't help, why he's looking at us and not doing anything. Yes, I had tremendous fight with God, after the war, during the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to pass the time while you were in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp? That is a very funny question, because you didn't have time to pass it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to maintain your dignity, or your humanness?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't hear you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to maintain your dignity or your humanness while in that situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] First of all, I never thought about myself then, an inmate. That's strange to say, you know. But I never thought I am imprisoned, even I knew was. I was normal, you know. I knew exactly what was going on. People were dying around me, and any minute I could have been shot, and I didn't know how long I will be alive. But somehow, I always escaped when I saw the smoke, what I did see all the time. But when that really hit me, and all the things depressed me or something-- I don't even know if that was the right expression-- then I close my eyes, and I was back in my thought in my home with my parents, and my sister, and brothers. I heard them talk to each other and to me. I came from a city where there were agriculture city-- you know, were fruit-growers. And the whole city in springtime was full of cherry blossom, and apple blossom, and so on. So the whole city was like a perfumed atmosphere. And I even smelled that in concentration camp. I escaped to that. Unfortunately, right now, and after the war, [SIGHS] I am still in two places at once. I am here in Los Angeles, thank God. I am very happy to be in America. But I am in Auschwitz also, every single day. I just can't escape that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to support each other in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked about the 10 girls who were together from the very beginning, making a pact that we are camp sisters and standing together five in a row every morning, two rows together in roll calls. We really did stay together. Whenever there was a selection, we tried to help each other. If someone was very weak, we put the person in the middle of the five as we were standing in line, so the SS shouldn't notice them, so-- well, we pinched each other's face, so we should looked healthier. And we always, always encouraged each other. We always told each other we would be free. Naturally, there was always one of us who was down and cried, didn't want to live anymore. But we didn't allow to either of us to come too low and give up. I had-- I have my girlfriend in New York, who had an aunt in New York at that time. And after the war, she wanted-- Gizko, her name-- to go to America. And she said she doesn't go-- just if I will be able to go. So I did receive the papers, also, to come to America. But that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of things did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I tell you that before? Because she-- in-- in Braunschweig, where we were-- that is another city in Germany when we came out from-- we were brought out from Auschwitz by the SS men to Germany. We did go there to work. And her 10 toes and fingers were frozen and raw, very painful. And she one day didn't want to come to work. So I wrapped her arm around mine arm, and I was pulling through the whole-- I don't know how many kilometers we were going to the working place-- with me, because I didn't want it to leave her behind. And one day, she didn't come, because she really couldn't anymore. She was sent to the so-called hospital, where they were just collecting people. And when you go on to the hospital, you never did return.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came back from work, and I asked, where is my friend, Gizko, they said she's next door in the hospital. That was a separate building. So when that was dark-- all day, that was air raid, but evening especially. And when that pitch dark, I came out from the place where I supposed to sleep, gone through a big hall where we every morning counted, in that time, and gone to the next house. By the door, there was an SS man standing. But somehow-- I don't know how-- he didn't see me and didn't hear me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did go to the next house. I talked to my girlfriend. I told her we will be free, and we will be together, just don't give up hope. Came back, and when I was in the middle of that hall, an SS man came-- I mean a light came into my face-- a flashlight-- and that was an SS man. And the blokova who was with him asked me where did I go. I told him the truth. He didn't say a word, but the blokova told me I will get it next day. I did tell you that. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back and talk more about your experience in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything-- I asked you earlier about things that you may have done to maintain your dignity while you were in the camp, any kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] activities, perhaps cultural activities, that you did, anything that you did to keep your sanity? OK. Why don't we stop and change tapes?
